item.title: Papua New Guineans in Middle East to be identified before assistance
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea nationals living and working in the Middle East are yet to be identified before assistance is sought from the Australian government and the United Arab Emirates (UAE). Foreign Affairs Secretary Elias Wohengu said given the dire situation, it was important for relatives and friends of PNG citizens stranded in the Middle East to contact the department through its state protocol and
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea nationals living and working in the Middle East are yet to be identified before assistance is sought from the Australian government and the United Arab Emirates (UAE).

Foreign Affairs Secretary Elias Wohengu said given the dire situation, it was important for relatives and friends of PNG citizens stranded in the Middle East to contact the department through its state protocol and consular division.

“Many governments are unable to carry out any eviction exercise at this point as all land borders and air space are closed, restricting the movement of people in and out of the Middle East,” he said.

“Evacuation itself would be a security risk under the present war scenario.”

Many PNG nationals travelling to the Middle East make their own arrangements and do not usually inform the embassy.

Wohengu said both the Australian government and the UAE were offering assistance to Papua New Guineans.

On Saturday, Israel and the United States launched air strikes on selected targets across Iran, killing Iran’s Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei and his immediate family.

Iran and its allies struck back targeting areas in the Middle East which are critical to the production of oil and natural gas, thus cutting off air and sea travel.

Foreign Affairs also said that the PNG embassy in Jerusalem would remain closed until the situation improved and it was safe to reopen.

Wohengu said given the situation, governments of the region had told service providers to accommodate stranded third-country nationals, including PNG citizens.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Heart surgery marks milestone
item.description: THE Port Moresby General Hospital (PMGH) has achieved a medical milestone with a successful open-heart surgery on a first female patient. Priscilla Alembo, a 46-year-old from Tari in Hela, underwent a life-saving mitral valve replacement on Monday.
item.content:encoded:
THE Port Moresby General Hospital (PMGH) has achieved a medical milestone with a successful open-heart surgery on a first female patient.

Priscilla Alembo, a 46-year-old from Tari in Hela, underwent a life-saving mitral valve replacement on Monday.

The procedure was conducted by a visiting cardiac team from Singapore’s National Heart Centre under SingHealth with PMGH’s heart specialists.

PMGH chief executive officer Dr Paki Molumi welcomed the SingHealth team on their third medical mission. He acknowledged the continued partnership and investment in training local specialists in Singapore, noting that the exchange programme initiated 10 years ago was successfully revived last year.

Dr Molumi also extended his appreciation to the Operation Open Heart Foundation for its unwavering support in advancing the skills and training of the local cardiac team through continuous professional development and exchange.

The complex surgery involved replacing a severely diseased mitral valve with either a mechanical or biological tissue valve.

Cardiothoracic surgeon Dr Noah Tapaua explained that Alembo was one of eight patients selected after extensive screening.

“Priscilla was one of eight patients selected after extensive screening.

“These patients were identified as suitable candidates who would significantly benefit from heart surgery,” Dr Tapaua said.

The operation was led by Dr Tapaua and Dr Zhu Ling, supported by a multidisciplinary team including anaesthesiologists, perfusionists, and intensive care nurses.

Professor Chua Yeow Leng, the team leader of the SingHealth delegation, expressed gratitude for the collaboration between PMGH and the various foundations.

“We are very fortunate to work alongside the staff of PMGH and Operation Open Heart to prepare for these complex cardiac procedures,” Chua said.

“Over the next four to five days, we will assess and determine the most appropriate surgical approach for each patient.”

The mission marks the second visit since the revival of the medical programme in 2025.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: World Bank to invest K1.8 billion in agriculture
item.description: THE World Bank is investing US$250 million (about K1.8 billion) into Papua New Guinea’s agriculture industry, according to two vice-presidents of the bank. Carlos Felipe Jaramillo (East Asia and the Pacific public sector) and Savesh Suri (international finance corporation vice-president Asia and the Pacific private sector) made the announcement in the presence of Prime Minister James Marape at the
item.content:encoded:
THE World Bank is investing US$250 million (about K1.8 billion) into Papua New Guinea’s agriculture industry, according to two vice-presidents of the bank.

Carlos Felipe Jaramillo (East Asia and the Pacific public sector) and Savesh Suri (international finance corporation vice-president Asia and the Pacific private sector) made the announcement in the presence of Prime Minister James Marape at the University of Goroka) yesterday.

“Creating better jobs for the people is a powerful pathway to get away from poverty. Creating and improving the quality of job is the mission of the World Bank group.

“This is what we work for,” Jaramillo said.

“We have a six-year partnership strategy with the PNG Government which focuses on helping businesses to grow so that they can create more, better jobs. Agriculture is at the core of this partnership. This is why we chose Goroka and the UOG to launch our joint effort to boost agriculture through agri-connect.”

He said that 80 per cent of PNG’s economy involved agriculture.

“Agriculture can improve the livelihood of the people and create more jobs,” he said.

“Investing in partnership and to create jobs in the value chain – from the farm gates to the processing factory. We want to connect the small farmers to markets.”

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Govt’s guns amnesty and buy-back aims to remove illegal firearms from citizens
item.description: THE Government is implementing a six-month guns amnesty and buy-back programme which aims to remove illegal guns from the hands of citizens around the country. Police Minister Sir John Pundari launched the programme in Enga last Friday.
item.content:encoded:
THE Government is implementing a six-month guns amnesty and buy-back programme which aims to remove illegal guns from the hands of citizens around the country.

Police Minister Sir John Pundari launched the programme in Enga last Friday.

He said the programme was endorsed by the National Executive Council, and would run from Feb 27 to National Repentance Day on Aug 26.

“This period will allow citizens to voluntarily surrender illegal firearms without penalty in exchange for cash payments during the amnesty window,” Sir John said.

“A grace period will run from Aug 27 to Sept 16.

“From Sept 17, the Royal Papua New Guinea Constabulary supported by the Papua New Guinea Defence Force will begin compulsory operations to recover remaining illegal weapons through intelligence driven policing and community cooperation.”

Sir John said the programme included voluntary amnesty, cash incentives under the buy-back scheme, rewards for informants whose information leads to weapon recoveries, and a 90-day update to the NEC to ensure transparency.

“Provincial Police Commanders will lead awareness campaigns with provincial governments, district development authorities, churches, NGOs and community leaders in a whole of government and whole of nation effort.”

Sir John said the priority was saving lives and reducing tribal violence.

“If this programme prevents even one massacre, it is not a reward, it is a strategic investment in human life,” he said.

However, Deputy Opposition Leader Keith Iduhu said the firearms amnesty and buy-back programme should be replaced with the 2005 Singirok Report framework.

Iduhu said the programme risked becoming a “revolving door” that fueled the country’s firearms crisis.

“Without a national iron-clad border and strict legislative enforcement, localised buy-backs often become a ‘revolving door’ where older weapons are traded for the capital to acquire newer, more lethal factory-made arms,” he said.

Iduhu highlighted significant legal concerns regarding the proposal to swap guns for business start-up funds.

“Legally, this approach is a departure from established criminal jurisprudence,” he said.

“Under the Firearms Act, possession of these weapons is an indictable offense. Transforming a criminal liability into a state-funded business asset contradicts the spirit of the Proceeds of Crime Act.

“We cannot allow the threat of violence to become a lever for economic gain, effectively bypassing the constitutional processes reserved for law-abiding SME entrepreneurs.”

Iduhu said an immediate review of the Singirok Report’s recommendations has been proposed, including:

* Establishment of a Firearms Council to take firearms administration out of the political sphere and into an independent regulatory body;

* Redirecting the K10 million buy-back budget to strengthen police intelligence units to locate and seize illegal caches;

* Implementing modern technology to track ammunition and state-owned weapons to prevent internal leakage; and,

* Information rewards, not weapon payments.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Pool of talent
item.description: THE excitement around the PNG Chiefs making their NRL debut in 2028 is growing across Papua New Guinea, and so is the following. With fewer than three years to go, discussions about recruitment and squad building are now in full swing.
item.content:encoded:
THE excitement around the PNG Chiefs making their NRL debut in 2028 is growing across Papua New Guinea, and so is the following.

With fewer than three years to go, discussions about recruitment and squad building are now in full swing.

The Chiefs’ management has made it clear that while signing experienced players is important to strengthen the squad for their first season, they will not overlook the wealth of PNG talent already competing overseas. Many Papua New Guinean internationals are currently contracted to clubs in the NRL, Super League, Betfred Championship, and the Queensland Hostplus Cup, offering the Chiefs a ready pool of players familiar with top-level rugby league.

These players have the potential to provide leadership and skill on the field for the Chiefs when the team makes its historic NRL entry.

The Super League also has a strong contingent of PNG internationals who can play a key role in supporting the Chiefs’ plans:

PNG Internationals in the Betfred Championship

London-based teams are also home to several PNG internationals who have showcased their skills in one of England’s toughest leagues:

PNG Internationals in the Queensland Hostplus Cup

Apart from top-tier competitions, Papua New Guinea internationals are also making their mark in the Queensland Hostplus Cup, a strong feeder system for NRL talent:

• Nixon Putt – Central Queensland Capras

• Valentine Richard – Central Queensland Capras

These players represent the future stars of Papua New Guinea rugby league.

Their inclusion in the Chiefs’ plans will ensure a balance of youth, skill, and local representation.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: DEM Cup kicks off in April
item.description: PAPUA New Guinea’s semi-professional rugby league competition, the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup, will kick off on April 12, with all 12 franchises across the Highlands, Momase, Southern, and New Guinea Islands regions deep in preseason preparations. Teams are holding training sessions, trial matches, and squad selections to ensure they hit the ground running when the season begins. Highlands franchises:
item.content:encoded:
PAPUA New Guinea’s semi-professional rugby league competition, the Digicel ExxonMobil Cup, will kick off on April 12, with all 12 franchises across the Highlands, Momase, Southern, and New Guinea Islands regions deep in preseason preparations.

Teams are holding training sessions, trial matches, and squad selections to ensure they hit the ground running when the season begins. Highlands franchises:

• HELA Wigmen: Hosted the Ben Hetra Memorial 9s Cup at the newly-built Wigmen Stadium in Tari. Andrew Andiki has been appointed head coach.

• ENGA Mioks: No official update yet, but preparations are believed to be underway.

• MENDI Muruks: Trial match planned between a select side from eight Southern Highlands affiliates and the Muruks squad. Announcements expected after trials.

• HAGEN Eagles: Recently staged a rugby league 9s tournament, with standout players selected for preseason. Former player Henry Wan takes over as head coach.

• WAGHI Tumbe: Completed the Tumbe 9s tournament. Preparations continue, although a head coach is yet to be named.

• GOROKA Lahanis: In full preseason mode, readying for the 2026 campaign.

• LAE Tigers: The defending champions are accelerating preparations, adding new talent as they aim to retain their title.

• MADANG Kandes: The new franchise is preparing for its debut season, using the Kande 9s tourney to select a preliminary squad.

• PORT Moresby Vipers: The capital city team is in full preparation mode.

• CENTRAL Dabaris: Nine weeks into preseason training under coach Solomon Kuluniasi, with trial matches expected soon.

•  GULF Isou: Focused on youth development, preparing to showcase emerging talent through its pathway programme.

•  RABAUL Gurias: Recently held an in-house trial match. Final squad announcement is expected soon. Wartovo Puara Jr returns as head coach, aiming to push beyond last year’s preliminary finals exit.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Hekari go down to Tahiti United
item.description: A FIRED-up PNG Hekari could not contain their Tahiti United opponents in their OFC Pro League campaign in Melbourne, Australia, yesterday, losing 1-2. The Round 1 match in Auckland was cancelled due to bad weather, and rescheduled to yesterday in Melbourne after all Round 3 fixtures.
item.content:encoded:
A FIRED-up PNG Hekari could not contain their Tahiti United opponents in their OFC Pro League campaign in Melbourne, Australia, yesterday, losing 1-2.

The Round 1 match in Auckland was cancelled due to bad weather, and rescheduled to yesterday in Melbourne after all Round 3 fixtures.

Hekari stepped onto the playing field with a strong line-up after their dominant win over Vanuatu on Saturday, and their hopes of securing a second against the Tahitians did not materialise.

Lack of concentration 16 minutes into the opening half turned bad for the Hekari lads. Tahiti United struck to lead 1-0.

Skipper Raymond Gunemba and John Orobulu had chances to equalise in the first 40 minutes but bad timing and inaccuracy got the better of them, trailing 0-1 at the break.

Tahiti returned with renewed energy and registered their second two minutes into the second half, leading 2-0.

Hekari reduced the deficit through an excellent combination play by Rex Naime and Joseph Joe (scoring), to trail 1-2.

Two penalties outside Tahiti’s 18-yard box went begging, leaving the Papua New Guineans trailing 1-2 at the final hooter.

Hekari coach Jerry Allan commended that lone goal from Joe and said it was a team effort and good combination up front that got them that pointer.

He said lack of concentration and silly errors in the first half allowed Tahiti to score early.

“We just need to fix our chances and work more on our finishes,” he said.

The next circuit will be in Honiara, Solomon Islands.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: IRC shoot down bankers to claim public service crown
item.description: INTERNAL Revenue Commission (IRC) are the champions of this year’s Public Servants Netball Association (PSNA) premier division competition. The taxwomen tipped the scoreboard at 24-21 over Bank of Papua New Guinea (BPNG) on Saturday at the Rita Flynn courts, Port Moresby.
item.content:encoded:
INTERNAL Revenue Commission (IRC) are the champions of this year’s Public Servants Netball Association (PSNA) premier division competition.

The taxwomen tipped the scoreboard at 24-21 over Bank of Papua New Guinea (BPNG) on Saturday at the Rita Flynn courts, Port Moresby.

The weekend’s grand showdown would go down as one of the fiercest premier division grand finals in recent PSNA history.

IRC, now known as the comeback queens, dethroned the three-time defending champions to claim their first crown at the premier level.

From the first whistle, both sides went out firing. Chasing their fourth consecutive title, BPNG stamped early authority in the opening quarter with a tight and disciplined defensive structure, shutting down IRC’s attacking rhythm to take a commanding 8-3 lead.

The second quarter saw a shift in momentum when IRC began lifting their intensity, increasing their tempo through the midcourt and applying relentless pressure.

Their persistence paid off as they clawed their way back into contention, narrowing the margin to 8-12 by halftime.

BPNG extended their lead in the third quarter, showcasing the composure of seasoned champions. However, IRC refused to back down and every turnover was contested fiercely, every pass fought for.

The scoreboard reflected the intensity, with BPNG holding a slender 16-13 advantage heading into the final break.

Then came the defining fourth quarter and IRC delivered under pressure.

Their goal defenders shut down crucial shooting lanes, the midcourt forced vital turnovers, and the goal-attack and goal-shooter displayed clinical precision when it mattered the most.

With seconds ticking away, and the crowd on its toes, IRC levelled the score and surged ahead in the dying moments until the final whistle.

A bittersweet triumph for the taxwomen after always falling short against the BPNG side, IRC finally overcame their rivals through sheer determination, belief, and relentless intensity, and a victory that marked a significant milestone for the 2025-26 season champions.

Meanwhile, president Mary Nauga thanked the PSNA netball family, the ones who give their time and energy to this association and to the sport that they all love, and the umpires for their essential role.

“This season we had an increase in our membership from 500+ to 1,000+ members, from seven divisions last season to 11 this season.

“I believe this is all due to the new inclusion of the mixed divisions with a total of 11 teams that registered,” she said.

Nauga acknowledged their sponsors and participating teams for a wonderful season and reminded them of the awards night that will be held at Apec Haus on Saturday.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Political party system needs to be fixed
item.description: AS talk of General Election 2027 (GE27) heats up, political parties will soon be out in full swing to prepare their candidates for the great race to gain the people’s favour for a seat in the National Parliament. It is important that people have a fair idea about the role of political parties and the system that governs them as they can seem quite complex to many.
item.content:encoded:
AS talk of General Election 2027 (GE27) heats up, political parties will soon be out in full swing to prepare their candidates for the great race to gain the people’s favour for a seat in the National Parliament.

It is important that people have a fair idea about the role of political parties and the system that governs them as they can seem quite complex to many.

Although PNG has been introduced to the western world for a little over 150 years from about 1873 on, political activism did not occur until fairly late in the piece.

It had been found to be nasty in colonies on the African continent so was very much discouraged in the Pacific.

Political parties such as Pangu, People’s Progress Party and Compas (precursor to United Party) began only after the first House of Assembly was elected in 1964.

Members of those parties tended to gravitate towards charismatic and influential leaders.

Pangu was synonymous in the 1970s and 1980s with Somare, PPP with Sir Julius Chan, National Party with Sir Iambakey Okuk and People’s Democratic Movement with Paias Wingti.

Parties were not founded on ideology or philosophy such as were distinguished in many other countries on how to structure society, distribute power and manage resources.

There was no out and out liberalism, socialism and communism or authoritarian or fascism ideologies. Policies tended to be drawn around development programs and regional based.

In such a loose field, party allegiances were very weak providing ammunition for members to move to and from political parties at will. It was an open door policy and governments and development suffered as a direct result of this fluid movement of members.

No political party or its leader lasted a full five-year term between the years 1977 clear through to 2002.

Mid-term changes of government through motions of no confidence or threats of them created a very unstable political atmosphere and no project or program could be expected to last a term of Parliament, much less across several terms.

In 2001, Sir Mekere Morauta, who himself took government when the sitting PM, Bill Skate resigned his office to avoid being forced out in a MoNC, finally halted the fluid nature of politics when he introduced and Parliament passed the Organic Law on Integrity of Political Parties and Candidates.

With the aid of this law, Grand Chief Sir Michael Somare’s National Alliance Party lasted an unprecedented full five year term between 2002 and 2007 and took office again after the 2007 general elections.

The result was stability and the stability fed economic growth.

Alas, this was not to last. In 2010 the Supreme Court rule on an application by the Fly River Provincial Government that several sections of the new law was unconstitutional.

The new law’s wings were fairly clipped and the result was instantaneous and horrendous.

Motions of no confidence and free movement of members between parties and camps became the norm again.

There is a pressing need now to address this weakness in the PNG political field now before the 2027 elections.

Last October, the current Registrar of Political Parties Emmanuel Pok highlighted said party-hopping by MPs added to the political instability and the country’s democracy being weakened because they were not being held accountable to the people.

“Political parties invest a lot of time and resources to have a candidate elected and it does not help them when MPs move from one party to another,” Pok said.

“We currently have 56 registered political parties, which gives MPs the choice to choose one different from than their own.

“There are too many political parties and we have to ensure that there can be a few strong ones that will make MPs remain in one party for their five-year term in Parliament.

“(But) we cannot hold leaders back to their political parties because of the 2010 Supreme Court decision.

“The Supreme Court, in its wisdom, made a decision that the elected leaders must exercise their own conscience to move from one party to another.”

Pok said amendments to the OLIPPAC, currently before Parliament for its third reading, would not only address political instability, but also improve the country’s electoral processes.

The key amendments proposed in the revised OLIPPAC Bill include:

Therefore, it is incumbent upon the current Parliament during its limited number of sittings this year to pass the OLIPPAC Bill promote political stability, support consistent governance and strengthen the current parliamentary system.

Such a task is of the utmost importance considering that GE27 is nigh.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: TB spread threatening Highlands
item.description: TUBERCULOSIS (TB) has long been a public health challenge in Papua New Guinea. Roughly 5-10 years ago, the disease was most commonly reported in coastal provinces such as Western, Gulf, and Central.
item.content:encoded:
TUBERCULOSIS (TB) has long been a public health challenge in Papua New Guinea.

Roughly 5-10 years ago, the disease was most commonly reported in coastal provinces such as Western, Gulf, and Central.

These areas, with difficult terrains and limited access to health services, became hotspots for transmission.

TB is now spreading rapidly in the Highlands, with Enga standing out as a province where all districts are affected.

This shift highlights how TB is not confined to one region – it adapts to social and environmental conditions, moving where health systems are weakest and communities most vulnerable.

Since, we are prone and vulnerable to sick TB, it is spreading.

The spread of TB in Enga and other Highlands provinces underscores the difficulties faced by communities in both urban and remote locations:

The Government has a crucial role to play in strengthening the TB response.

International donor agencies have been instrumental in funding TB, HIV/AIDS, and other essential medications in PNG.

Their continued support is vital because:

From a layman’s perspective, the message is simple but urgent: TB is spreading in Enga and across the Highlands, and people need help.

The Government must act quickly, and donor agencies must continue their support.

Without sustained intervention, TB will not only persist, but may become more resistant and harder to treat.

TB in PNG is not just a medical issue – it is a social and economic challenge that affects families, communities, and the nation’s future.

The spread into the Highlands, particularly Enga, is a warning sign.

Health workers, government authorities, and donor agencies must work together to ensure that every patient receives timely diagnosis, continuous treatment, and proper education.

Only then can PNG hope to control and eventually eliminate TB.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Make capital city safe and clean
item.description: THE people and community leaders of Port Moresby should give their full support to the recent call by Governor Powes Parkop to reintroduce the Vagrancy Act. Our capital city must not drift further into disorder.
item.content:encoded:
THE people and community leaders of Port Moresby should give their full support to the recent call by Governor Powes Parkop to reintroduce the Vagrancy Act.

Our capital city must not drift further into disorder.

Public harassment, petty crime, public drunkenness, and intimidation in public places affect families, businesses, churches, and visitors.

A capital city must reflect national pride, not fear.

At the same time, enforcement must be fair, lawful, and balanced.

The purpose of the law should not be to punish poverty.

It should be to address behaviour that threatens public safety and peace.

Clear definitions must guide enforcement so that citizens are protected from arbitrary action.

I suggest a practical, future-focused solution to city hall, parliament, government departments, and civil society.

The Royal Papua New Guinea Constabulary must be properly trained, equipped, and supervised.

Independent oversight mechanisms should monitor implementation to prevent abuse or selective targeting.

Second, create real social support systems.

Temporary shelters, counselling services, rehabilitation for drug and alcohol abuse, and job skills training must operate alongside enforcement.

Churches, non-governmental organisations, and the private sector can play a key role in reintegration programmes.

Many street-related issues involve unemployed young people.

Skills training, small business grants, apprenticeships, and structured community work programmes can offer alternatives to street life.

Public education campaigns in schools, settlements, and workplaces should encourage cleanliness, respect for public property, and shared responsibility for maintaining order.

Our end goal is clear: a peaceful, clean, safe, and cooperative capital city – one that can proudly stand among the best cities in the Pacific region.

Order must walk together with compassion.

Strong leadership must walk together with community support.

If we unite around balanced solutions, Port Moresby can become a model capital built on dignity, discipline, and hope.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Prosecution for assault on journalist
item.description: THE Correctional Services (CS) officers (law enforcement officials) who allegedly assaulted the female reporter from The National must face the full brunt of the law. Where in their animalistic brain cells did they decided that journalist were public enemy number one.
item.content:encoded:
THE Correctional Services (CS) officers (law enforcement officials) who allegedly assaulted the female reporter from The National must face the full brunt of the law.

Where in their animalistic brain cells did they decided that journalist were public enemy number one.

The CS personnel thought that allegedly assaulting a journalist the other day was fine.

Well they have something coming for them.

These culprits must be prosecuted, judged and sentenced back to Bomana where they rightfully belong. There is no ifs and no buts.

The journalists and the press are the voice of the people in our communities – they have always been.

As they say hang them high – hang them low.

Save my name, email, and website in this browser for the next time I comment.

item.title: Urban elites, rural exclusion and politics of growth
item.description: Neglect of the rural population is frequently attributed to globalisation. But treating globalisation as the sole culprit averts attention from deeper policy failures that have widened spatial schisms within countries. This post reflects on an article, “The Urban-Rural Gap and the Dilemma of Governance” by Anirudh Krishna, on that issue.
item.content:encoded:
Neglect of the rural population is frequently attributed to globalisation. But treating globalisation as the sole culprit averts attention from deeper policy failures that have widened spatial schisms within countries.

This post reflects on an article, “The Urban-Rural Gap and the Dilemma of Governance” by Anirudh Krishna, on that issue.

Although the article was published over a decade ago, its arguments are strikingly relevant today.

In the article, Krishna argues that the divide in today’s world is no longer only between countries but increasingly within countries.

Wealth, opportunity and political influence are concentrated through the process of urban agglomeration and the creation of urban elites while rural areas stagnate in the background.

Fast-growing economies such as China, India, Indonesia and South Africa now contain both those who are elite and those who are poor by global standards.

Krishna shows that poverty rates are consistently higher in rural areas.

For instance, in Malawi, 57 per cent of the rural population lives below the poverty line, compared with 17 per cent of urban residents.

In Zambia, the figures are 78 per cent rural versus 28 per cent urban; in Vietnam, it’s 22 per cent versus five per cent in rural and urban areas, respectively.

Over the decade these numbers might have changed, but still the argument made then holds equally for Papua New Guinea and its Melanesian neighbours, as World Bank data shows (Figure 1).

In Ethiopia, 97 per cent of urban residents have access to improved drinking water, compared to only 42 per cent of rural residents.

In India, 60 per cent of city residents have improved sanitation facilities versus only 25 per cent in rural areas.

In PNG, rural sanitation is at 15 per cent whereas urban sanitation rates hover at 49 per cent.

 Educational attainment is similarly unequal.

In Pakistan, 61 per cent of urban residents complete secondary education, compared with half of that figure for rural residents.

Even when years of schooling are similar, learning outcomes are poorer in rural schools.

Rural areas are poorer, but urban areas are more productive.

According to Krishna, this results in a governance dilemma: should governments prioritise poor rural households or globally competitive households in capital cities?

In theory, democratic systems should balance both.

Despite making up only a small proportion of the total population, urban elites’ preferences shape policy priorities, public investment decisions and national development narratives. 

Governments build airports, information superhighways and elite universities in urban centres, but rural schools, health clinics, roads and internet access are underfunded.

All this results, Krishna argues, in rural populations’ being locked into informal, insecure and sometimes sordid forms of work.

When economic mobility out of rural poverty is blocked, control of the state becomes the most reliable route to wealth.

I can’t help but think of my own country.

During the first general election in 1977, two years after independence, under 900 persons contested in PNG’s national elections.

The last general election in 2022 saw around 3,619 candidates contest the national election.

Even after accounting for population growth, competition for political power has intensified.

There were 31 contestants per 100,000 persons in 1977.

That number rose to 35.5 contestants per 100,000 persons in 2022 – an increase of about 15 per cent. 

Ask any Papua New Guinean about their future aspirations and chances are high they will say that they want to become a politician.

item.title: Chasm between anti-corruption pledges and practicalities
item.description: THE complexities and contradictions of implementing anti-corruption initiatives in a Pacific Island context were on full display in Suva this week. Fiji’s principal anti-corruption body, the Fiji Independent Commission Against Corruption (Ficac), laid charges against Fijian and British national Charlie Charters, alleging two counts of aiding and abetting a whistleblower and the unlawful publicatio
item.content:encoded:
THE complexities and contradictions of implementing anti-corruption initiatives in a Pacific Island context were on full display in Suva this week.

Fiji’s principal anti-corruption body, the Fiji Independent Commission Against Corruption (Ficac), laid charges against Fijian and British national Charlie Charters, alleging two counts of aiding and abetting a whistleblower and the unlawful publication of official Commission information on Facebook between Nov 2 and Dec 14 last year.

Notably, this development follows Ficac’s last January recommendation to a parliamentary standing committee that a Whistleblower Protection Act be established, emphasising the need to shield informants, including civil servants, from retaliation.      

Former journalist Charters was remanded in custody over the weekend before appearing in court on Monday (Feb 23), where he was granted bail.

According to media reports, he said he declined an offer that would have required him to reveal the identity of a whistleblower in exchange for being able to proceed with a planned flight to Sydney.

Charters had previously reported on alleged improper appointments and malpractice within Ficac, which are accusations that remain contested.

The Charters’ case coincides with two significant anti-corruption milestones this month: a sixth anniversary event for the Teieniwa Vision in Suva and the release of Transparency International’s 2025 Corruption Perceptions Index (CPI).

The CPI assesses 182 countries and territories and is widely regarded as a key global measure of perceived public sector corruption.

And the Teieniwa Vision, adopted by Pacific leaders in 2020, explicitly supports whistleblowers, the right to information and the role of independent civil society and responsible media in anti-corruption efforts. The charges against Charters have prompted discussion about the persistent gap in the Pacific between anti-corruption commitments and their practical delivery.

Taken together, the CPI report and the Teieniwa Vision anniversary call for reflection on the state of corruption in the Pacific and the effectiveness of current anti-corruption efforts.

At the anniversary event in Suva, co-hosted by the Pacific Islands Forum (PIF) and the United Nations Office on Drugs and Crime (UNODC), Kiribati President Taneti Maamau, in a message delivered on his behalf, recalled that the Vision arose from a “profound realisation” that corruption was “a rising tide threatening the very foundations of our sovereign nations”.

Such warnings by Maamau and others are arguably more urgent today than ever before.

In addition to historic, pre-existing challenges, the Pacific now faces the growing danger of transnational crime networks and drug cartels, which thrive in regions with weak institutions and enforcement gaps.

This concern was raised during the high-level panel discussion marking the anniversary.

The PIF policy adviser for regional security, Neumi Vakadewabuka, identified governance weaknesses as a key factor driving the expanding presence of organised and transnational crime syndicates in the region.

He noted recent regional surveys indicating that the integrity of immigration and customs systems, law enforcement agencies and broader governance structures across the Pacific is being significantly tested by these syndicates.

Vakadewabuka’s observations are reinforced by a series of major drug seizures across the region.

Last month, Tahitian authorities intercepted a vessel carrying 96 bales of cocaine.

On the same day, Fijian police seized more than 100 bags of suspected cocaine in Tavua, in the Western Division.

The head of the UNODC Pacific office, Marie Pegie Cauchois, agreed on the major threats posed by organised crime.

“The UNODC continues to highlight that corruption creates the openings organised crime needs, while integrity is what closes those doors,” said Cauchois, also a panellist at the discussion.

“The strengthening of transparent institutions, border governance and oversight frameworks is therefore central to safeguarding the region,” she added.

Against this backdrop of rising organised crime, the latest CPI findings take on a grimmer significance.

 The report notes that corruption remains a “serious threat in every part of the world” and that any signs of progress are “limited”.

However, data indicate that corruption is especially pervasive in developing regions such as the Pacific, where, as the Teieniwa Vision underscores, the impact is felt most acutely among vulnerable groups.

The CPI scores countries on a scale of 0 (highly corrupt) to 100 (very clean).

The lowest-scoring Pacific Island country was Papua New Guinea at 26 points – an alarming 19 points below the Asia-Pacific average.

Vanuatu scored 47, Solomon Islands 44 and Fiji 55, indicating little movement in recent years.

While the Teieniwa Vision’s anti-corruption strategy reflects the right intent – stronger practices, adoption of UN frameworks and adherence to the rule of law – the challenge lies in sustained commitment and implementation.

Despite the Vision’s support for the right to information and protection for genuine whistleblowers, hardly any Pacific Island countries have enacted such legislation.

item.title: Sri Lankan Panditha receives award for services to PNG
item.description: UNIVERSITY of Papua New Guinea lecturer Panditha Bandara, from Sri Lanka, and now a permanent resident of PNG, received the Independence King’s Medal this month for his 24 years of service to the country. “I came to PNG in February, 2002. From that day and onwards, I (have been) serving, and available for services. So this medal coincides with that.”
item.content:encoded:
UNIVERSITY of Papua New Guinea lecturer Panditha Bandara, from Sri Lanka, and now a permanent resident of PNG, received the Independence King’s Medal this month for his 24 years of service to the country.

“I came to PNG in February, 2002. From that day and onwards, I (have been) serving, and available for services. So this medal coincides with that.”

Panditha, 76, arrived in PNG in 2002 with his wife, Padma. They have a daughter.

He joined the Goroka Technical College in 2002, as a senior lecturer and deputy principal. He started the Diploma in Accounting programme there.

He was invited to join UPNG in 2007. Now he is a senior lecturer and head of the accounting division at the University of PNG School of Business and Public Policy.

He is also the Honorary Consul for Sri Lanka in PNG.

One of the things he cherishes is the annual Christmas donation to the Cheshire home in December.

“For the past two years, we’ve donated nearly K30,000 worth of goods, glossaries and even cash.”

As Sri Lanka’s Honorary Consul, he has been doing his utmost to strengthen bilateral relationship with PNG.

“The most recent (move) was last week when the Somare Institute of Leadership and Governance and National Institute of Business Management in Sri Lanka signed a technical corporation agreement.”

Panditha had also organised to bring human eye corneas to PNG every month and donate them to the Port Moresby General Hospital.

“These live eye corneas are transplanted to the people who have vision impairment.”

His services are not confined to the university and teaching.

“I go beyond that. I have also been a council member of the Certified Practicing Accounts PNG for nine consecutive years. I’m still serving the accounting profession.”

In addition, he authored 11 books in Business Studies, Accounting and Economics for PNG students “from Grades 9 to 12 and university level”.

He was not expecting any recognition for his services.

“I did not think about any recognition in this manner. I really worked for my pleasure and I don’t expect any medals or any recognition like this, but it came.

“In fact, in 2017, I was recognised for Logohu. Now, I am receiving this 50 th anniversary King’s Independence Medal.”

He thanks his wife Padma for supporting him.

I did not think about any recognition in this manner. I really worked for my pleasure and I don’t expect any medals or any recognition like this, but it came.”

“We’ve been married for 47 years. Her support is remarkable. Without it, I cannot go beyond my office work and extra work. I’m grateful to her.”

“He is always committed to service in his life. It has been recognised, so it will give him the boost to do more. When he needs to do something, I provide everything for him.  When he is doing his work, I never call him to say we go there and there.” 

Panditha had experienced living in different cultural environments before coming to PNG.

“Before I came, some people were talking about PNG this and that. But I said, I am a risk-taker. I have international experience in living in different cultures. PNG is another culture. So it is not a culture shock.”

One thing he has noticed since arriving in PNG is how the country “has changed for the better”.

“When we came here, we didn’t see these supermarkets, buildings and roads and all these things. Now you can see them. It is more or less close to the developed suburb of a developed country.

“Also the (higher) education level and professionalism of young people. These things I have noted and I have contributed to as well. I have directly or indirectly taught between 10,000 and 15,000 young people.”

He encourages young Papua New Guineans to be committed to their goals.

“My advice is: Commit yourself to achieve your goal. You must have a goal. Commit yourself to achieve it.”

He thanks the Government for recognising his services since he arrived in 2002.
